question.content: In the figure, point C lies on semicircle O with AB as its diameter. Draw AC and BC. The tangent to semicircle O at C meets the extension of AB at point D. On arc AC, choose point E so that arc EC equals arc BC. Connect BE, and let it intersect AC at F. If sin D = sqrt(3)/2 and BD = 1, find the radius of semicircle O and the length of BF. Answer format requirement: <expr>. Strictly forbidden: any extra text beyond this format.

step_1.content: Let the semicircle radius be r and set O as the origin. Writing C as (r*cos(theta), r*sin(theta)), the tangent at C meets the x-axis at D, and sin D = cos(theta) = sqrt(3)/2 gives theta = 30 degrees.
step_1.key_point: Translate the tangent-angle condition into the point angle of C.

step_3.content: Then D has x-coordinate r/cos(theta), so BD = r*(sec(theta)-1) = r*(2/sqrt(3)-1) = 1. Hence r = sqrt(3)/(2-sqrt(3)) = 3+2*sqrt(3).
step_3.key_point: Use the tangent-intercept length BD to solve the radius.

step_5.content: Because arc EC equals arc BC, point E is at 60 degrees, so E=(r/2,sqrt(3)*r/2). The lines AC and BE intersect at F, and solving the two line equations gives BF = 2*sqrt(3).
step_5.key_point: Convert equal arcs into E, then use the intersection of AC and BE.
